title: Camden, New Jersey

infobox.value: Victor Carstarphen ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(New_Jersey) "Democratic Party (New Jersey)"), term ends December 31, 2029)[[2]](#cite_note-Mayor-2)
208th of 565 in state 7th of 37 in county[[7]](#cite_note-CensusArea-7)

is a [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). It is part of the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") metropolitan region.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The city was incorporated on February 13, 1828.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) Camden has been the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Camden County[[22]](#cite_note-CountyMap-22) since the county's formation on March 13, 1844.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) The city derives its name from [Charles Pratt, 1st Earl Camden](/wiki/Charles_Pratt,_1st_Earl_Camden "Charles Pratt, 1st Earl Camden").[[23]](#cite_note-Hutchinson-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Gannett-24) Camden is made up of over 20 neighborhoods,[[25]](#cite_note-camconnect.org-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Camden_Facts-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Locality_Search-27) and is part of the [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") region of the state.

**Camden** is a [city](/wiki/City_(New_Jersey) "City (New Jersey)") in [Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"). It is part of the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") metropolitan region.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The city was incorporated on February 13, 1828.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) Camden has been the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Camden County[[22]](#cite_note-CountyMap-22) since the county's formation on March 13, 1844.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) The city derives its name from [Charles Pratt, 1st Earl Camden](/wiki/Charles_Pratt,_1st_Earl_Camden "Charles Pratt, 1st Earl Camden").[[23]](#cite_note-Hutchinson-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Gannett-24) Camden is made up of over 20 neighborhoods,[[25]](#cite_note-camconnect.org-25)[[26]](#cite_note-Camden_Facts-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Locality_Search-27) and is part of the [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey") region of the state.

The initial growth of Camden industrially is often credited to the "big three" employers: [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor"), [Campbell's Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company") and [New York Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/New_York_Shipbuilding_Corporation "New York Shipbuilding Corporation"). The "big three" felt compelled to move away from Camden in the mid-to-late-20th century as they could find cheaper workers elsewhere.[[28]](#cite_note-Camden,_New_Jersey-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Camden_Transformed-29) Though the city has declined in recent decades since the decline of heavy industry in the area and [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") to the suburbs, the city has made efforts to revitalize itself through various infrastructure and community projects.

Projects such as the redevelopment of the waterfront area brought three tourist attractions to the area: the [Battleship New Jersey Museum and Memorial](/wiki/Battleship_New_Jersey_Museum_and_Memorial "Battleship New Jersey Museum and Memorial"), the [Freedom Mortgage Pavilion](/wiki/Freedom_Mortgage_Pavilion "Freedom Mortgage Pavilion") and the [Adventure Aquarium](/wiki/Adventure_Aquarium "Adventure Aquarium").[[30]](#cite_note-Attractions-30) The city is the home of [Rutgers University–Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Camden "Rutgers University–Camden"), which was founded as the South Jersey Law School in 1926,[[31]](#cite_note-History-31) and [Cooper Medical School of Rowan University](/wiki/Cooper_Medical_School_of_Rowan_University "Cooper Medical School of Rowan University"), which opened in 2012. Camden also houses both [Cooper University Hospital](/wiki/Cooper_University_Hospital "Cooper University Hospital") and [Virtua Our Lady of Lourdes Hospital](/wiki/Virtua_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes_Hospital "Virtua Our Lady of Lourdes Hospital"). [Camden County College](/wiki/Camden_County_College "Camden County College") and [Rowan University](/wiki/Rowan_University "Rowan University") also have campuses in downtown Camden. The "[eds](/wiki/University "University") and [meds](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital")" institutions account for roughly 45% of Camden's total employment.[[32]](#cite_note-camden.rutgers.edu-32)

Once known for violent crime, the restructuring of the police force in 2013 has been credited for its decline. As of January 2021, violent crime was down 46% from its high in the 1990s and at the lowest level since the 1960s. Overall crime reports in 2020 were down 74% compared to 1974, the first year of uniform crime-reporting in the city.[[33]](#cite_note-WHYY-33)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/History_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "History of Camden, New Jersey")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

The city traces back to local indigenous [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), who are believed to have inhabited this area 13–15,000 years prior to the first European settlers.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-2024-34)

### Settlement years (1623–1701)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Settlement years (1623–1701)")]

Between 1623 and 1627, Captain [Cornelius Jacobsen May](/wiki/Cornelius_Jacobsen_May "Cornelius Jacobsen May"), an officer with the [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") and first director of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), established [Fort Nassau](/wiki/Fort_Nassau_(South_River) "Fort Nassau (South River)"), where the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") meets Big Timber Creek, which is today known as [Brooklawn](/wiki/Brooklawn,_New_Jersey "Brooklawn, New Jersey"). In 1633, [David Pietersen De Vries](/wiki/David_Pietersz._de_Vries "David Pietersz. de Vries"), a Dutch commander, was sailing up the Delaware River when he came across Natives in control of the fort. The settlers that had been left at the fort had decided to return to [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (*Today* [Manhattan, New York](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")). [Wouter van Twiller](/wiki/Wouter_van_Twiller "Wouter van Twiller"), Governor of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), restored Fort Nassau. He was accused of extravagant spending in the fort's reconstruction. The settlement subsequently sparked competition from European Settlers over control of the fur trade in the area.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-CamdenHistory2-36) The fort was used by the Dutch until around 1650 or 1651 when it was decided that it was too far up the river to be of any value. The buildings and stockades were demolished and Wouter van Twiller assigned Arent Corssen to find a place for another fort.

The British first had a presence in the area in 1634. On June 21, 1634, Sir [Edmund Ployden](/wiki/Edmund_Plowden_(colonial_governor) "Edmund Plowden (colonial governor)") was given a charter from [King Charles I of England](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") for all territory that lies between [New England](/wiki/New_England_Colonies "New England Colonies") and [Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland"). After the [Restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") in 1660, previous claims were largely overwritten, the land around Camden was then controlled by different nobles serving under [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") that those associated with Sir Edmund Ployden.[[36]](#cite_note-CamdenHistory2-36)

In 1664, the Duke of York had the King Charles II create the new colony for [Lord John Berkeley](/wiki/John_Berkeley,_1st_Baron_Berkeley_of_Stratton "John Berkeley, 1st Baron Berkeley of Stratton") and [Sir George Carteret](/wiki/George_Carteret "George Carteret"). It was named the [Province of New Jersey](/wiki/Province_of_New_Jersey "Province of New Jersey") after George Carteret; in 1649, he was [Governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Jersey "Lieutenant Governor of Jersey") of the [Isle of Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey"). Lord John Berkeley kept his share of New Jersey from 1664 until 1674, when he sold it to two [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [John Fenwick](/wiki/John_Fenwick_(Quaker) "John Fenwick (Quaker)") and [Edward Byllynge](/wiki/Edward_Byllynge "Edward Byllynge"). This due to political difficulties between him, Carteret, and [Governor of New York](/wiki/List_of_colonial_governors_of_New_York "List of colonial governors of New York") [Richard Nicolls](/wiki/Richard_Nicolls "Richard Nicolls"), as well as financial difficulties. Governor Richard Nicolls had objected to the Province of New Jersey as he had exercised control over the area prior under the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York").[[36]](#cite_note-CamdenHistory2-36) After Edward Byllynge suffered a bankruptcy and having issues with his creditors, [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), one of the creditors, was chosen to be arbitrator. They argued that he funded the purchase of Lord John Berkeley's share of the Province of New Jersey with funds that were justly due to them. It was decided that Fenwick was entitled to 10% of the share, while 90% would be controlled by trustees that are chosen for the benefit of the creditors of Edward Byllynge, who were mostly Quakers themselves. The trustees were chosen to be William Penn, [Gawen Laurie](/wiki/Gawen_Lawrie "Gawen Lawrie") and Nicholas Lucas. The goal was to have the trustees sell the territory to colonists so that the creditors of Edward Byllynge would be made whole. It was also hoped that Quaker may be motivated to emigrate to this territory. At the time, the [Society of Friends](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") were flirting with the idea of "new country", where they could practice their religious beliefs and not be shamelessly persecuted. In 1676, the Quakers decided to form a colony, spittling the previous colony in two, [East Jersey](/wiki/East_Jersey "East Jersey") and [West Jersey](/wiki/West_Jersey "West Jersey") for the Quakers. Quakers settled in the area at the end of the 17th century and the start of the 18th century, drawn by promises of religious freedom, fairer taxation and more representation in government.[[34]](#cite_note-:0-2024-34)

### Colonial (1702–1775)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Colonial (1702–1775)")]

The Quakers expansion, consumption of resources, along with the introduction of alcohol and disease, led to a decline in the Lenape population. The development of a ferry system along the Delaware River bolstered trade between [Fort Nassau](/wiki/Fort_Nassau_(South_River) "Fort Nassau (South River)") and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia").[[36]](#cite_note-CamdenHistory2-36) Through ferries, families like Coopers and the [Kaighns](/wiki/Kaighn_family "Kaighn family") were able to establish settlements in surrounding areas. In 1773, Jacob Cooper played a significant role in developing the area which is today known as Camden, named after Charles Pratt, the Earl of Camden.[[36]](#cite_note-CamdenHistory2-36)

### Post-colonial (1776–1827)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-colonial (1776–1827)")]

Throughout the Revolutionary War, there were several skrimishes and other effects of the war felt by locals. Development was impeded for the villiage due to the revolution, as Camden was held by the British along with Philadelphia across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Founding and early years (1828–1890)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Founding and early years (1828–1890)")]

In the 19th century Camden underwent significant changes, transitioning from a hub of transportation to a growing city. Camden was incorporated as a city on February 13, 1828, from portions of [Newton Township](/wiki/Newton_Township,_Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Newton Township, Camden County, New Jersey"), while the area was still part of [Gloucester County](/wiki/Gloucester_County,_New_Jersey "Gloucester County, New Jersey"). In 1832, Camden Township was created as a [township](/wiki/Township_(New_Jersey) "Township (New Jersey)") [coextensive](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/coextensive "wiktionary:coextensive") with Camden City. The township existed until it was repealed in 1848.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) Camden Township was established in 1832 which was the same area as Camden City until it was reduced in 1848.[[21]](#cite_note-Story-21) In 1830, the [Camden and Amboy Railroad Company](/wiki/Camden_and_Amboy_Railroad_and_Transportation_Company "Camden and Amboy Railroad and Transportation Company") was chartered in Camden, which connected ferry terminals from New York City to Philadelphia via rail. The railroad ended in Camden's Waterfront, where passengers would be ferried across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") to arrive in Philadelphia. Similarly to Camden's inception, transportation was a huge catalyst in its growth—the railroads opening in 1834 led to an increase in population and commerce.[[38]](#cite_note-:4-38)

### Industrial growth (1891–1950)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Industrial growth (1891–1950)")]

At the turn 20th Century, industry grew rapidly at the hands of companies such as the [Victor Talking Machine Company](/wiki/Victor_Talking_Machine_Company "Victor Talking Machine Company") (later [RCA Victor](/wiki/RCA_Victor "RCA Victor")), [New York Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/New_York_Shipbuilding_Corporation "New York Shipbuilding Corporation"), and [Campbell Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company"). These were major employers in Camden, at times employing tens of thousands in and outside of Camden.[[39]](#cite_note-:18-39)[[40]](#cite_note-:19-40)[[41]](#cite_note-:20-41) Its location on the Delaware River made it ideal to launch ships.

Camden also experienced dramatic shifts in its population demographic. Immigration from Eastern Europe made them the leading ethnic group by 1920, whereas it had previously been German, British, and Irish immigrants.[[42]](#cite_note-:22-42) In 1926, a bridge connecting New Jersey and Pennsylvania opened, which was formally named the [Benjamin Franklin Bridge](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Bridge "Benjamin Franklin Bridge") in 1956.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The project cost $37 million (equivalent to $673 million in 2025), split evenly between New Jersey and Pennsylvania.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The goal was to reduce ferry traffic between Philadelphia and Camden.

[Camden Central Airport](/wiki/Camden_Central_Airport "Camden Central Airport") opened in 1929 and was used by several major airlines until [Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport") opened in 1939 and brought air service across the Delaware River. The airport officially closed in April 1957, with a shopping center to be constructed on the 50-acre (20 ha) site.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

During the 1930s, Camden faced economic decline in the face of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). It was due to Camden's thriving industry that they did not go bankrupt. The United States role in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") made the New York Shipbuilding Company the largest and most productive ship yard in the world.[[46]](#cite_note-46) World War II caused African American migration in and around Camden from the south as there was a need for factory workers for the war effort. Subsequently, Camden became ethnically and religiously segregated.[[47]](#cite_note-AfterTheFall3-47) On July 17, 1951, the [Delaware River Port Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_Port_Authority "Delaware River Port Authority"), a bi-state agency, was created to promote trade and better coordinate transportation between the two cities of Camden and Philadelphia.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

### Industrial decline (1951–1991)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Industrial decline (1951–1991)")]

However, by the 1950s, manufacturing began to slow, causing industries to relocate and employment to dwindle.[[47]](#cite_note-AfterTheFall3-47) In contrast to the growth and industrialization Camden experienced in the early 1900s, there came a drop in population and industry further into the 20th century.[[47]](#cite_note-AfterTheFall3-47) Having reached its peak number of manufacturing jobs in 1950, by 1982 it was a quarter of what it had been. After World War II, Campbell's Soup Company and RCA Victor decentralized their production efforts in Camden.[[49]](#cite_note-49) This Capital Flight was an attempt to avoid an increase in labor wages which unionized workers were fighting for.[[28]](#cite_note-Camden,_New_Jersey-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Camden_Transformed-29) The New York Shipbuilding Company, a major contributor of naval units during World War II, shut down in 1967 due to low demand and mismanagement.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

During this period there was a large amount of [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight"), in which white residents moved to surrounding suburbs in search of safety and economic opportunity.[[47]](#cite_note-AfterTheFall3-47) Neighborhoods were further divided by the construction of the [North-South Freeway](/wiki/Interstate_676#New_Jersey "Interstate 676"); 1,289 families were displaced by the highway, 85% of which were nonwhite families.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Along with this, civil unrest grew resulting in riots. Police brutality and crime were at an all-time high which further exacerbated Camden's problems.

### Revitalization (1992–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revitalization (1992–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Cooper_Street-Rutgers_University_Station.jpg/250px-Cooper_Street-Rutgers_University_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cooper_Street-Rutgers_University_Station.jpg)

The [River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(NJ_Transit) "River Line (NJ Transit)")'s [Cooper Street–Rutgers University station](/wiki/Cooper_Street%E2%80%93Rutgers_University_station "Cooper Street–Rutgers University station") stop

Efforts to revitalize Camden began in 1980 with Mayor [Randy Primas](/wiki/Randy_Primas "Randy Primas"). In an attempt to generate income for the city, he pursued initiatives such as the construction of a riverfront state-prison and a trash-to-steam incinerator which received substantial opposition from residents.[[52]](#cite_note-Unruh-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) With the election of [Milton Milan](/wiki/Milton_Milan "Milton Milan") as Camden's next mayor, he declared the city bankrupt, which resulted in $60 million of aid and the state's assumption of Camden's finances.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Another notable revitalization effort was the establishment of non-profit organization, The Parkside Business and Community In Partnership, which occurred in 1993 and is active today.[[55]](#cite_note-:022-55)

#### Redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Redevelopment")]

Main article: [Redevelopment of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Redevelopment_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Redevelopment of Camden, New Jersey")

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Camden](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Camden "List of tallest buildings in Camden")

[![An image of Camden Towers, American Water Headquarters and 11 Cooper St Apartments](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/The_End_of_Cooper_St..jpg/250px-The_End_of_Cooper_St..jpg)](/wiki/File:The_End_of_Cooper_St..jpg)

Camden Towers (left), American Water Headquarters (center), and 11 Cooper St Apartments (right) in Camden

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/South_Jersey_Gas_Camden_NJ.JPG/250px-South_Jersey_Gas_Camden_NJ.JPG)](/wiki/File:South_Jersey_Gas_Camden_NJ.JPG)

The former Camden Downtown Branch of the Camden County Library

Redevelopment as an idea has loomed over the city since the 1980s, when Mayor Primas started looking for projects to be able to revitalize with the loss of several foundational industries in the preciding decades. In 2013 the [New Jersey Economic Development Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Economic_Development_Authority "New Jersey Economic Development Authority") introduced incentives for companies to relocate to Camden.[[56]](#cite_note-The_Star-Ledger-56) Other projects include the redevelopment of the Waterfront, the construction of the [Philadelphia 76ers Training Complex](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers_Training_Complex "Philadelphia 76ers Training Complex"), and the [Subaru of America](/wiki/Subaru_of_America "Subaru of America")'s headquarters.

#### 2020s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020s")]

In recent years, Camden has transitioned from a manufacturing industry to an economy focused on education and healthcare. The Eds-and-Meds Industry has become the largest source of employment in Camden—with institutions such as [Cooper University Hospital](/wiki/Cooper_University_Hospital "Cooper University Hospital"), [Rowan University](/wiki/Rowan_University "Rowan University"), [Rutgers-Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Camden "Rutgers University–Camden"), [Camden County College](/wiki/Camden_County_College "Camden County College"), Virtua, [Our Lady of Lourdes Medical Center](/wiki/Virtua_Our_Lady_of_Lourdes_Hospital "Virtua Our Lady of Lourdes Hospital"), and CAMcare.[[57]](#cite_note-Rurcbog.com-57) In October 2025, the [New Jersey Economic Development Authority (NJDEA)](/wiki/New_Jersey_Economic_Development_Authority "New Jersey Economic Development Authority") issued a Request for Expressions of Interest (RFEOI) to solicit development proposals of 16 acres along the [North Camden](/wiki/North_Camden "North Camden") waterfront on the site of the former prison, which had been demolished in 2009, as well as part of the Weeks Marine site; enacting on a redevelopment place in place since 2014.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![A community sign near Camden's Cooper Grant neighborhood showcasing the cities unofficial tagline "A City Invincible"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Coopergrant.jpg/250px-Coopergrant.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coopergrant.jpg)

"A City Invincible" sign, the city's official tagline, near Camden's [Cooper Grant](/wiki/Cooper_Grant,_Camden "Cooper Grant, Camden") neighborhood

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/USS_New_Jersey_Night.jpg/250px-USS_New_Jersey_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_New_Jersey_Night.jpg)

The Battleship [*USS New Jersey*](/wiki/USS_New_Jersey_(BB-62) "USS New Jersey (BB-62)") on the Camden waterfront in 2010

Camden's role as an industrial city gave rise to distinct neighborhoods and cultural groups that have affected the growth and decline of the city over the course of the 20th century. Camden is also home to historic landmarks detailing its rich history in literature, music, social work and industry such as the [Walt Whitman House](/wiki/Walt_Whitman_House "Walt Whitman House"),[[60]](#cite_note-NEWS01_2009-60) the Walt Whitman Cultural Arts Center, the Rutgers–Camden Center for the Arts and the [Camden Children's Garden](/wiki/Camden_Children%27s_Garden "Camden Children's Garden"). Others include the Camden County Historical Society, which document the city and surrounding area's history. It was built in 1899 as a place for those who find anything that links to one's heritage and for other educational purposes.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Camden's cultural history has been greatly affected by both its economic and social position over the years. From 1950 to 1970, industry plummeted, resulting in close to 20,000 jobs being lost for Camden residents.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This mass unemployment as well as social pressure from neighboring townships caused an exodus of citizens, mostly white. This gap was filled by new African American and Latino citizens and led to a restructuring of Camden's communities. The number of White citizens who left to neighboring towns such as [Collingswood](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey") or [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey") left both new and old African American and Latino citizens to re-shape their community. To help in this process, numerous not-for-profit organizations such as Hopeworks or the Neighborhood Center were formed to facilitate Camden's movement into the 21st century.[[47]](#cite_note-AfterTheFall3-47)

### Community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Community")]

See also: [African-Americans in Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/African-Americans_in_Camden,_New_Jersey "African-Americans in Camden, New Jersey") and [Hispanic and Latino community of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_community_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Hispanic and Latino community of Camden, New Jersey")

[The Black Community](/wiki/The_Black_Community_of_Camden,_NJ "The Black Community of Camden, NJ") has been one of the city's foundations since its founding in 1828 and have contributed heavily to the city's culture. [Corinne's Place](/wiki/Corinne%27s_Place "Corinne's Place") is a Black-owned soul food restaurant located in Camden, New Jersey. Corinne Bradley-Powers opened the restaurant on Haddon Avenue in 1989.[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63) [The Hispanic and Latino Community](/wiki/The_Hispanic_and_Latino_Community_of_Camden,_NJ "The Hispanic and Latino Community of Camden, NJ") in the city has increased heavily in the past twenty years, but have had a long history in Camden. Puerto Rican Unity for Progress is a multi-service, community-based organization that is located in Camden and serves the Hispanic community who reside in the city. The organization was established in 1976 and opened its physical location at 437 Broadway Street in Camden in June 1978.

### Arts and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Arts and entertainment")]

Main article: [Arts and entertainment in Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Arts_and_entertainment_in_Camden,_New_Jersey "Arts and entertainment in Camden, New Jersey")

The Arts and Entertainment have always been presence in the city. In the early 20th century, Camden became a hub of music and innovation in entertainment with the presence of the Victor Talking Machine Company (later RCA Victor). It is the birthplace of celebrities such as singers [Russ Columbo](/wiki/Russ_Columbo "Russ Columbo") and [Lola Falana](/wiki/Lola_Falana "Lola Falana"). Today, Camden is home to individuals and groups that help bolster the arts in the city.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Service-pnp-habshaer-nj-nj0000-nj0010-photos-111470pv.jpg/250px-Service-pnp-habshaer-nj-nj0000-nj0010-photos-111470pv.jpg)](/wiki/File:Service-pnp-habshaer-nj-nj0000-nj0010-photos-111470pv.jpg)

Newton Monthly Meeting of the Religious Society of Friends

Camden has religious institutions including many churches and their associated non-profit organizations and community centers such as the Little Rock Baptist Church in the Parkside section of Camden, First Nazarene Baptist Church, Kaighn Avenue Baptist Church, and the Parkside United Methodist Church. Other congregations that are active now are Newton Monthly Meeting of the Religious Society of Friends on Haddon Avenue and Cooper Street and the Masjid on Mechanic Street.

The first [Scientology church](/wiki/Church_of_Scientology "Church of Scientology") was incorporated in December 1953 in Camden by [L. Ron Hubbard](/wiki/L._Ron_Hubbard "L. Ron Hubbard"), his wife [Mary Sue Hubbard](/wiki/Mary_Sue_Hubbard "Mary Sue Hubbard"), and John Galusha.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Service-pnp-vrg-04800-04876v.jpg/250px-Service-pnp-vrg-04800-04876v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Service-pnp-vrg-04800-04876v.jpg)

Sacred Heart Catholic Church on Broadway and Ferry Ave in Camden

Father Michael Doyle, the pastor of Sacred Heart Catholic Church located in South Camden, has played a large role in Camden's spiritual and social history. In 1971, Doyle was part of [the Camden 28](/wiki/The_Camden_28 "The Camden 28"), a group of anti-Vietnam War activists who planned to raid a draft board office in the city. This is noted by many as the start of Doyle's activities as a radical 'Catholic Left'. Following these activities, Monsignor Doyle went on to become the pastor of Sacred Heart Church, remaining known for his poetry and activism.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Monsignor Doyle and the Sacred Heart Church's main mission is to form a connection between the primarily white suburban surrounding areas and the inner-city of Camden.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

In 1982, Father Mark Aita of Holy Name of Camden founded the St. Luke's Catholic Medical Services. Aita, a medical doctor and a member of the [Society of Jesus](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus"), created the first medical system in Camden that did not use rotating primary care physicians. Since its conception, St. Luke's has grown to include Patient Education Classes as well as home medical services, aiding over seven thousand Camden residents.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Philanthropy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Philanthropy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Cathedral_Kitchen_in_Camden_New_Jersey.jpg/250px-Cathedral_Kitchen_in_Camden_New_Jersey.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_Kitchen_in_Camden_New_Jersey.jpg)

Cathedral Kitchen in Camden

The city has long had a history of philanthropy and charity, dating back to its founding. The city's founding families were quakers that were very interested in charitable causes like the care of orphans and helping runaway slaves. They were members of the [Society of Friends](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") whose members were the likes of William Penn. In 1865, the Society of Friends founded the [Camden Home for Friendless Children](/w/index.php?title=Camden_Home_for_Friendless_Children&action=edit&redlink=1 "Camden Home for Friendless Children (page does not exist)"). Since that home was segregated, the Society of Friends opened the [West Jersey Colored Orphanage](/wiki/West_Jersey_Colored_Orphanage "West Jersey Colored Orphanage") in 1874.

Camden has a variety of non-profit Tax-Exempt Organizations aimed to assist city residents with a wide range of health and social services free or reduced charge to residents. Camden City, having one of the highest rates of poverty in New Jersey, fueled residents and local organizations to develop organizations aimed to provide relief to its citizens. As of the 2000 Census, Camden's income per capita was $9,815. This ranking made Camden the poorest city in the state of New Jersey, as well as one of the poorest cities in the United States.[[71]](#cite_note-Speer_399–408-71) Camden also has one of the highest rates of childhood poverty in the nation.[[71]](#cite_note-Speer_399–408-71)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Campbellsoupheadquarters.jpg/250px-Campbellsoupheadquarters.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campbellsoupheadquarters.jpg)

[Campbell Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company")'s headquarters in Camden

About 45% of employment in Camden is in the "eds and meds" sector, providing educational and medical institutions.[[32]](#cite_note-camden.rutgers.edu-32)

In 2018, the city had an average residential property tax bill of $1,710, the lowest in the county, compared to an average bill of $6,644 in Camden County and $8,767 statewide.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Urban enterprise zone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Urban enterprise zone")]

Portions of Camden are part of a joint [Urban Enterprise Zone](/wiki/Urban_Enterprise_Zone "Urban Enterprise Zone"). The city was selected in 1983 as one of the initial group of 10 zones chosen to participate in the program.[[74]](#cite_note-74) In addition to other benefits to encourage employment within the Zone, shoppers can take advantage of a reduced 3.3125% [sales tax](/wiki/Sales_tax "Sales tax") rate (half of the 6.625% rate charged statewide) at eligible merchants.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Established in September 1988, the city's Urban Enterprise Zone status expires in December 2023.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

The UEZ program in Camden and four other original UEZ cities had been allowed to lapse as of January 1, 2017, after Governor [Chris Christie](/wiki/Chris_Christie "Chris Christie"), who called the program an "abject failure", vetoed a compromise bill that would have extended the status for two years.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In May 2018, Governor [Phil Murphy](/wiki/Phil_Murphy "Phil Murphy") signed a law that reinstated the program in these five cities and extended the expiration date in other zones.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

## Geography and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Geography and architecture")]

According to the [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city had a total area of 10.34 square miles (26.78 km2), including 8.92 square miles (23.10 km2) of land and 1.42 square miles (3.68 km2) of water (13.75%).[[7]](#cite_note-CensusArea-7)[[79]](#cite_note-GR1-79)

Camden borders [Collingswood](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey"), [Gloucester City](/wiki/Gloucester_City,_New_Jersey "Gloucester City, New Jersey"), [Oaklyn](/wiki/Oaklyn,_New_Jersey "Oaklyn, New Jersey"), [Pennsauken Township](/wiki/Pennsauken_Township,_New_Jersey "Pennsauken Township, New Jersey") and [Woodlynne](/wiki/Woodlynne,_New_Jersey "Woodlynne, New Jersey") in Camden County, as well as [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82) Just offshore of Camden is [Pettys Island](/wiki/Pettys_Island "Pettys Island"), which is part of Pennsauken Township. The [Cooper River](/wiki/Cooper_River_(New_Jersey) "Cooper River (New Jersey)") (popular for boating) flows through Camden, and Newton Creek forms Camden's southern boundary with Gloucester City.

### Waterfront

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Waterfront")]

Main article: [Camden Waterfront](/wiki/Camden_Waterfront "Camden Waterfront")

See also: [Redevelopment of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Redevelopment_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Redevelopment of Camden, New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Camden_waterfront_skyline.jpg/250px-Camden_waterfront_skyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:Camden_waterfront_skyline.jpg)

The Camden waterfront seen from across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") in 2005

Historically, the Waterfront has always been a foundational part and major hub of the city. It was home to the New York Shipbuilding Company Shipyards until 1968. Since the 1990s, the Waterfront began a beacon of revitalization for the city. The city's waterfront, along the Delaware River is highlighted by its three main attractions, the [USS *New Jersey*](/wiki/USS_New_Jersey_(BB-62) "USS New Jersey (BB-62)"), the [Freedom Mortgage Pavilion](/wiki/Freedom_Mortgage_Pavilion "Freedom Mortgage Pavilion"), and the [Adventure Aquarium](/wiki/Adventure_Aquarium "Adventure Aquarium").[[30]](#cite_note-Attractions-30) The waterfront is also the headquarters for [Catapult Learning](/wiki/Catapult_Learning "Catapult Learning"), the [Philadelphia 76ers Training Complex](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers_Training_Complex "Philadelphia 76ers Training Complex"), [American Water](/wiki/American_Water_Works "American Water Works"). Camden has two generally recognized neighborhoods located on the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") waterfront, Central and South. Other attractions at the Waterfront are the Wiggins Park Riverstage and Marina, One Port Center, The Victor Lofts, the [Walt Whitman House](/wiki/Walt_Whitman_House "Walt Whitman House"),[[60]](#cite_note-NEWS01_2009-60) the Walt Whitman Cultural Arts Center, the Rutgers–Camden Center for the Arts, the [Camden Children's Garden](/wiki/Camden_Children%27s_Garden "Camden Children's Garden"), Cooper's Poynt Park (former site of [Riverfront State Prison](/wiki/Riverfront_State_Prison "Riverfront State Prison")).

### Port

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Port")]

Main article: [Port of Camden](/wiki/Port_of_Camden "Port of Camden")

On the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River"), with access to the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), the [Port of Camden](/wiki/Port_of_Camden "Port of Camden") handles [break bulk](/wiki/Break_bulk_cargo "Break bulk cargo"), [bulk cargo](/wiki/Bulk_cargo "Bulk cargo"), as well as some containers. Terminals fall under the auspices of the [South Jersey Port Corporation](/wiki/South_Jersey_Port_Corporation "South Jersey Port Corporation") as well as private operators such as Holt Logistics/[Holtec International](/wiki/Holtec_International "Holtec International"). The port receives hundreds of ships moving international and domestic cargo annually and is one of the USA's largest shipping centers for wood products, cocoa and perishables.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Housing")]

The most common type of home in Camden is [rowhouse](/wiki/Terraced_house "Terraced house"), similar to those in the neighboring city of Philadelphia. [Saint Josephs Carpenter Society (SJCS)](/w/index.php?title=Saint_Josephs_Carpenter_Society&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saint Josephs Carpenter Society (page does not exist)") is a non profit that has rehabilitated 500 homes throughout the city.

Camden contains the United States' first federally funded [planned community](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community") for working class residents, [Yorkship Village](/wiki/Yorkship_Village "Yorkship Village") (now called Fairview).[[84]](#cite_note-84) The village was designed by [Electus Darwin Litchfield](/wiki/Electus_Darwin_Litchfield "Electus Darwin Litchfield"), who was influenced by the ["garden city"](/wiki/Garden_city_movement "Garden city movement") developments popular in England at the time.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

In 2013, Cherokee Investment Partners had a plan to redevelop north Camden with 5,000 new homes and a shopping center on 450 acres (1.8 km2). Cherokee dropped their plans in the face of local opposition and the slumping real estate market.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88) They are among several companies receiving [New Jersey Economic Development Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Economic_Development_Authority "New Jersey Economic Development Authority") (EDA) tax incentives to relocate jobs in the city.[[89]](#cite_note-The_Star-Ledger2-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Climate")]

Camden has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa* in the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification")) with hot summers and cool to cold winters.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Education")]

### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Public schools")]

Main article: [Camden City School District](/wiki/Camden_City_School_District "Camden City School District")

Camden's public schools are operated by the [Camden City School District](/wiki/Camden_City_School_District "Camden City School District"). The district is one of 31 former [Abbott districts](/wiki/Abbott_district "Abbott district") statewide that were established pursuant to the decision by the [New Jersey Supreme Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Supreme_Court "New Jersey Supreme Court") in *Abbott v. Burke*[[92]](#cite_note-92) which are now referred to as "SDA Districts" based on the requirement for the state to cover all costs for school building and renovation projects in these districts under the supervision of the [New Jersey Schools Development Authority](/wiki/New_Jersey_Schools_Development_Authority "New Jersey Schools Development Authority").[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) As of the 2020–21 school year, the district, composed of 19 schools, had an enrollment of 7,553 students and 668.0 classroom teachers (on an [FTE](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") basis), for a [student–teacher ratio](/wiki/Student%E2%80%93teacher_ratio "Student–teacher ratio") of 11.3:1.[[95]](#cite_note-NCES-95)

High schools in the district (with 2020–21 enrollment data from the [National Center for Education Statistics](/wiki/National_Center_for_Education_Statistics "National Center for Education Statistics")[[96]](#cite_note-96)) are
[Brimm Medical Arts High School](/wiki/Brimm_Medical_Arts_High_School "Brimm Medical Arts High School")[[97]](#cite_note-97) (175; 9–12),
[Camden Big Picture Learning Academy](/wiki/MetEast_High_School "MetEast High School")[[98]](#cite_note-98) (196; 6–12),
[Camden High School](/wiki/Camden_High_School_(New_Jersey) "Camden High School (New Jersey)")[[99]](#cite_note-99) (347; 9–12),
[Creative Arts Academy](/wiki/Creative_Arts_Academy "Creative Arts Academy")[[100]](#cite_note-100) (290; 6–12),
[Eastside High School](/wiki/Eastside_High_School_(Camden,_New_Jersey) "Eastside High School (Camden, New Jersey)")[[101]](#cite_note-101) (784; 9–12) and
Pride Academy[[102]](#cite_note-102) (63; 6–12).[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

### Charter and renaissance schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Charter and renaissance schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/KIPP_Cooper_Norcross_Lanning_Square_Primary_and_MIddle_School.jpg/250px-KIPP_Cooper_Norcross_Lanning_Square_Primary_and_MIddle_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:KIPP_Cooper_Norcross_Lanning_Square_Primary_and_MIddle_School.jpg)

KIPP Cooper Norcross Lanning Square Primary and Middle School

In 2012, The Urban Hope Act was signed into law, allowing renaissance schools to open in Trenton, Newark, and Camden. The renaissance schools, run by charter companies, differed from charter schools, as they enrolled students based on the surrounding neighborhood, similar to the city school district. This makes renaissance schools a hybrid of charter and public schools. This is the act that allowed [Knowledge Is Power Program](/wiki/KIPP_(organization) "KIPP (organization)") (KIPP), Uncommon Schools, and Mastery Schools to open in the city.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

Under the renaissance charter school proposal, the Henry L. Bonsall Family School became Uncommon Schools Camden Prep Mt. Ephraim Campus, East Camden Middle School has become part of Mastery Charter Schools, Francis X. Mc Graw Elementary School and Rafael Cordero Molina Elementary School have become part of the Mastery charter network. The J.G Whittier Family school has become part of the KIPP Public Charter Schools as KIPP Cooper Norcross Academy.
Students were given the option to stay with the school under their transition or seek other alternatives.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

In the 2013–14 school year, Camden city proposed a budget of $72 million to allot to charter schools in the city. In previous years, Camden city charter schools have used $52 million and $66 million in the 2012–2013 and 2013–2014 school years, respectively.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

March 9, 2015, marked the first year of the new Camden Charter Schools open enrollment. Mastery and Uncommon charter schools did not meet enrollment projections for their first year of operation by 15% and 21%, according to Education Law Center.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

In October 2016, Governor Chris Christie, Camden Mayor Dana L. Redd, Camden Public Schools Superintendent Paymon Rouhanifard, and state and local representatives announced a historical $133 million investment of a new Camden High School Project.[[110]](#cite_note-110) The new school is planned to be ready for student occupancy in 2021. It would have 9th and 12th grade.

As of 2019, there are 3,850 Camden students enrolled in one of the city's renaissance schools, with 4,350 Camden students are enrolled one of the city's charter schools.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Combined, these students make up approximately 55% of the 15,000 students in Camden.

### Private education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Private education")]

Holy Name School,[[114]](#cite_note-114) Sacred Heart Grade School,[[115]](#cite_note-115) and St. Joseph Pro-Cathedral School (founded in 1894)[[116]](#cite_note-116) are K–8 elementary schools operating under the auspices of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Camden "Roman Catholic Diocese of Camden").[[117]](#cite_note-117) They operate as four of the five schools in the Catholic Partnership Schools, a post-parochial model of Urban Catholic Education.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Higher education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Ru-camden-campus.jpg/250px-Ru-camden-campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ru-camden-campus.jpg)

[Rutgers University–Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Camden "Rutgers University–Camden") and the [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") skyline (background)

The University District, adjacent to the downtown, is home to the following institutions:

* [Camden County College](/wiki/Camden_County_College "Camden County College") – one of three main campuses; the college first came to the city in 1969 and constructed a campus building in Camden in 1991.[[119]](#cite_note-119)
* [Rowan University at Camden](/wiki/Rowan_University "Rowan University"), [satellite campus](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus") – the Camden campus began with a program for teacher preparation in 1969 and expanded with standard college courses the following year and a full-time day program in 1980.[[120]](#cite_note-120)
* [Cooper Medical School of Rowan University](/wiki/Cooper_Medical_School_of_Rowan_University "Cooper Medical School of Rowan University") (opened 2012)[[121]](#cite_note-121)
* [Rutgers University–Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_University%E2%80%93Camden "Rutgers University–Camden") – the Camden campus, one of three main sites in the university system, began as South Jersey Law School and the College of South Jersey in the 1920s and was merged into Rutgers in 1950.[[122]](#cite_note-122)
  + Camden College of Arts & Sciences[[123]](#cite_note-123)
  + School of Business – Camden[[124]](#cite_note-124)
  + [Rutgers School of Law-Camden](/wiki/Rutgers_School_of_Law-Camden "Rutgers School of Law-Camden")[[125]](#cite_note-125)
* [University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey](/wiki/University_of_Medicine_and_Dentistry_of_New_Jersey "University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey") (UMDNJ)
  + Affiliated with [Cooper University Hospital](/wiki/Cooper_University_Hospital "Cooper University Hospital")
* [Coriell Institute for Medical Research](/wiki/Coriell_Institute_for_Medical_Research "Coriell Institute for Medical Research")[[126]](#cite_note-126)
  + Affiliated with Cooper University Hospital
  + Affiliated with [Rowan University](/wiki/Rowan_University "Rowan University")
  + Affiliated with University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Libraries")]

The city was once home to two [Carnegie libraries](/wiki/List_of_Carnegie_libraries_in_New_Jersey "List of Carnegie libraries in New Jersey"), the [Main Building](/wiki/Camden_Free_Public_Library_Main_Building "Camden Free Public Library Main Building")[[127]](#cite_note-library-127) and the [Cooper Library in Johnson Park](/wiki/Cooper_Library_in_Johnson_Park "Cooper Library in Johnson Park").[[128]](#cite_note-128) The city's once extensive library system, beleaguered by financial difficulties, threatened to close at the end of 2010, but was incorporated into the county system.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) The [main branch](/wiki/South_Jersey_Gas,_Electric_and_Traction_Company_Office_Building "South Jersey Gas, Electric and Traction Company Office Building") closed in February 2011,[[131]](#cite_note-131) and was later reopened by the county in the bottom floor of the Paul Robeson Library at Rutgers University.[[132]](#cite_note-132)

Camden also has three [academic libraries](/wiki/Academic_libraries "Academic libraries"); The [Paul Robeson](/wiki/Paul_Robeson "Paul Robeson") Library at Rutgers University-Camden serves Rutgers undergraduate and graduate students, as well as students from the Camden campuses of Camden County College and Rowan University. Rutgers Law School has a [law library](/wiki/Law_library "Law library") and Cooper Medical School at Rowan has a medical library.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Sports")]

### Camden Athletic Complex

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Camden Athletic Complex")]

The Camden Athletic Complex (former site of [Campbell's Field](/wiki/Campbell%27s_Field "Campbell's Field")) which was completed in 2022. it contains a baseball field, track and field area, soccer field, and lacrosse field. The [Camden Riversharks](/wiki/Camden_Riversharks "Camden Riversharks") were an American professional baseball team based in Camden, which played out of the former Campbell's Field. An investment totaling $15 million, planned to be split evenly between Rutgers and the city of Camden, will reportedly develop the area into a recreational complex for the city, as well as accommodations for the university's [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") sports teams.[[133]](#cite_note-133)

### Philadelphia 76ers training facility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Philadelphia 76ers training facility")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Phil76ersTrainFacilCamden1.tif/lossless-page1-250px-Phil76ersTrainFacilCamden1.tif.png)](/wiki/File:Phil76ersTrainFacilCamden1.tif)

The training facility of the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") in Camden

The team found a property at the [Camden Waterfront](/wiki/Camden_Waterfront "Camden Waterfront"). An $82 million grant was approved by the New Jersey Economic Development Authority to begin construction of the training facility in Camden, and was scheduled to break ground in October 2014.[[134]](#cite_note-76Grant-134) The grant was somewhat controversial in that it saves the 76ers organization from paying any property taxes or fees that would be accrued by the building over its first decade. Vocal opponents of the facility claim that the site has now joined a list of large companies or industries that are invited to Camden with significant monetary incentive, at great expense to local tax payers as a form of [corporate welfare](/wiki/Corporate_welfare "Corporate welfare").[[135]](#cite_note-135) Based on contingent hiring, the grant was to be paid out over 10 years, with the facility scheduled to host practices by 2016.[[134]](#cite_note-76Grant-134) The training facilities include the two full-size courts, as well as a weight room, full hydrotherapy room, Gatorade Fuel Bar, full players-only restaurant and personal chef, medical facilities, film room, and full locker room.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Government")]

Main article: [Government of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Government_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Government of Camden, New Jersey")

Further information: [Political corruption in Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Political_corruption_in_Camden,_New_Jersey "Political corruption in Camden, New Jersey") and [Mayors of Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Mayors_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Mayors of Camden, New Jersey")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Camden_city_hall.jpg/250px-Camden_city_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Camden_city_hall.jpg)

[Camden's City Hall](/wiki/Camden_City_Hall "Camden City Hall") opened in 1931.

Since July 1, 1961, the city has operated within the [Faulkner Act](/wiki/Faulkner_Act "Faulkner Act"), formally known as the Optional Municipal Charter Law, under a [Mayor-Council](/wiki/Faulkner_Act_(mayor%E2%80%93council) "Faulkner Act (mayor–council)") form of government.[[5]](#cite_note-DataBook-5)[[136]](#cite_note-:5-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137) Since 1994, the city has been divided into four [council districts](/wiki/Ward_(United_States) "Ward (United States)"); previously, the entire council was elected at-large.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Elected officials are elected to four-year terms in odd-numbered years, with the mayor and the three at-large seats up for election together and the four ward seats up for vote two years later.[[139]](#cite_note-139)

As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the Mayor of Camden is Democrat Victor Carstarphen, whose term of office ends December 31, 2029.[[2]](#cite_note-Mayor-2) Members of the City Council are Council President [Angel Fuentes](/wiki/Angel_Fuentes "Angel Fuentes") (D, 2029; at large), Vice President Sheila Davis (D, 2029; at large), Arthur Barclay (D, 2027; Ward 1), Christopher R. Collins (D, 2027; Ward 2), Falio Leyba-Martinez (D, 2027; Ward 3), Jannette Ramos (D, 2027; Ward 4) and Noemi G. Soria-Perez (D, 2029; at large).[[140]](#cite_note-140)[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-Camden2025-142)[[143]](#cite_note-Camden2023-143)

It was home to the Norcross brothers, three brothers who have dominated Southern Jersey democratic politics for the past 25 years, until legal troubles in 2024 led them to take a backseat. The city has had its struggles with corruption throughout its political history. Three Camden mayors have been jailed for corruption: [Angelo Errichetti](/wiki/Angelo_Errichetti "Angelo Errichetti"), Arnold Webster, and [Milton Milan](/wiki/Milton_Milan "Milton Milan").[[144]](#cite_note-:3-144)

### Federal, state and county representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Federal, state and county representation")]

Camden is located in [New Jersey's 1st Congressional District](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_1st_congressional_district "New Jersey's 1st congressional district")[[145]](#cite_note-PCR2012-145) and is part of [New Jersey's 5th state legislative district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_5th_legislative_district "New Jersey's 5th legislative district").[[146]](#cite_note-Districts2011-146)[[147]](#cite_note-LWV2019-147)[[148]](#cite_note-148)

For the [119th United States Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), [New Jersey's 1st congressional district](/wiki/New_Jersey%27s_1st_congressional_district "New Jersey's 1st congressional district") is represented by [Donald Norcross](/wiki/Donald_Norcross "Donald Norcross") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), Camden).[[149]](#cite_note-149)[[150]](#cite_note-150)New Jersey is represented in the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Cory Booker](/wiki/Cory_Booker "Cory Booker") ([Newark](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey"), term ends 2027) and [Andy Kim](/wiki/Andy_Kim "Andy Kim") ([Moorestown](/wiki/Moorestown,_New_Jersey "Moorestown, New Jersey"), term ends 2031).[[151]](#cite_note-151)

[Camden County](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey") is governed by a [Board of County Commissioners](/wiki/Board_of_County_Commissioners_(New_Jersey) "Board of County Commissioners (New Jersey)") composed of seven members chosen [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in partisan elections for three-year terms on a staggered basis by the residents of the county, with either two or three seats up for election each year as part of the November general election. At a reorganization meeting held in January after each election, the newly constituted Board of Commissioners selects one member to serve as Director and another as Deputy Director, each serving a one-year term in that role.[[152]](#cite_note-About-152) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit), Camden County's Commissioners are:
Commissioner Director Louis Cappelli Jr. ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), [Collingswood](/wiki/Collingswood,_New_Jersey "Collingswood, New Jersey"), 2026),[[153]](#cite_note-153)
Commissioner Deputy Director Edward T. McDonnell (D, [Pennsauken Township](/wiki/Pennsauken_Township,_New_Jersey "Pennsauken Township, New Jersey"), 2025),[[154]](#cite_note-154)
Virginia Ruiz Betteridge (D, [Runnemede](/wiki/Runnemede,_New_Jersey "Runnemede, New Jersey"), 2025),[[155]](#cite_note-155)
Almar Dyer (D, Pennsauken Township, 2027),[[156]](#cite_note-156)
Melinda Kane (D, [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey"), 2027),[[157]](#cite_note-157)
Jeffrey L. Nash (D, [Winslow Township](/wiki/Winslow_Township,_New_Jersey "Winslow Township, New Jersey"), 2027),[[158]](#cite_note-158) and
Jonathan L. Young Sr. (D, [Berlin Township](/wiki/Berlin_Township,_New_Jersey "Berlin Township, New Jersey"), 2026).[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[152]](#cite_note-About-152)[[160]](#cite_note-TemplateCamden2022-160)[[161]](#cite_note-TemplateCamden2021-161)[[162]](#cite_note-TemplateCamden2020-162)

Camden County's constitutional officers are:
Clerk [Pamela Rosen Lampitt](/wiki/Pamela_Rosen_Lampitt "Pamela Rosen Lampitt") (D, Cherry Hill, 2029)[[163]](#cite_note-163)[[164]](#cite_note-164)
Sheriff Chuck Billingham (D, [Gloucester City](/wiki/Gloucester_City,_New_Jersey "Gloucester City, New Jersey"), 2027)[[165]](#cite_note-165)[[166]](#cite_note-166) and
Surrogate Michelle Gentek-Mayer (D, [Gloucester Township](/wiki/Gloucester_Township,_New_Jersey "Gloucester Township, New Jersey"), 2025).[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Politics")]

Camden has historically been a stronghold of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"). As of November 6, 2018, there were 42,264 registered voters in the city of Camden.[[170]](#cite_note-170) As of March 23, 2011, there were 43,893 registered voters in Camden, of which 17,403 (39.6%) were registered as [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), 885 (2.0%) were registered as [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") and 25,601 (58.3%) were registered as [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Unaffiliated_(New_Jersey) "Unaffiliated (New Jersey)").[[171]](#cite_note-171) All [Camden mayors](/wiki/Mayors_of_Camden,_New_Jersey "Mayors of Camden, New Jersey") since 1935 have been Democrats. The last Republican Camden mayor was Frederick von Nieda, who only sat in office for a year.[[172]](#cite_note-172) During his second term, Obama visited Camden in 2015 and said that "Hold you up as a symbol of promise for the nation. This city is on to something, no one is suggesting that the job is done," the president said. "It's still a work in progress."[[173]](#cite_note-173) In the [2012 presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), Democrat [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") was seeking reelection and was challenged by [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney"). The city overwhelmingly voted for Obama in the biggest Democratic landslide in Camden's history.

## Emergency Services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Emergency Services")]

### Camden Fire Department (CFD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Camden Fire Department (CFD)")]

[![A fire department in Camden, NJ](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Camden_NJ_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg/250px-Camden_NJ_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Camden_NJ_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg)

The Fire Administration Building on N 3rd Street in Camden, NJ

[![One of the fire engines in the garage at N 3rd St Fire Administration Building.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Fire_Truck_at_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg/250px-Fire_Truck_at_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fire_Truck_at_Fire_Administration_Building.jpg)

Fire Engine at N 3rd St. Fire Administration Building.

Officially organized in 1869, the [Camden Fire Department (CFD)](/wiki/Camden_Fire_Department "Camden Fire Department") is the oldest paid fire department in New Jersey and is among the oldest paid fire departments in the United States.[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184)[[185]](#cite_note-185) The Camden Fire Department currently operates out of five fire stations, organized into two battalions. Each battalion is commanded by a battalion chief, who in turn reports to a deputy chief. The CFD currently operates five engine companies, one squad (rescue-pumper), three ladder companies, and one rescue company, as well as several other special, support, and reserve units. The department's fireboat is docked on the Delaware River. Since 2010, the Camden Fire Department has suffered severe economic cutbacks, including company closures and staffing cuts.[[186]](#cite_note-186)

### Camden County Police Department (CCPD)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Camden County Police Department (CCPD)")]

Main article: [Camden County Police Department](/wiki/Camden_County_Police_Department "Camden County Police Department")

Further information: [Crime in Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Crime_in_Camden,_New_Jersey "Crime in Camden, New Jersey")

On May 1, 2013, [Camden Police Department](/wiki/Camden_Police_Department_(defunct) "Camden Police Department (defunct)") was disbanded due to a union contract that made it financially impossible to keep officers on the street. The [Camden County Police Department](/wiki/Camden_County_Police_Department "Camden County Police Department") was formed to succeed the Camden Police Department. Camden County's Police Department brought in 25 new officers to train in neighborhoods in hopes they could regain the trust of local communities.[[187]](#cite_note-:02-187) Because of the reorganized force in 2013, the number of cops in the streets has increased, and spread throughout Camden. Camden's new police force began patrolling in tandem, speaking with residents, and driving patrol cars.[[188]](#cite_note-:32-188) A *CNN* report proposed that Camden might be a national model for what [police abolition](/wiki/Police_abolition_movement "Police abolition movement") or "[defunding the police](/wiki/Defund_the_police "Defund the police")" could look like.[[189]](#cite_note-:37-189)

## Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Crime")]

Camden once had a national reputation for its violent crime rates, although recent years have seen a significant drop in violent crime, with 2017 seeing the lowest number of homicides in three decades.[[190]](#cite_note-:16-190)

| Camden | |
| --- | --- |

| [Robbery](/wiki/Robbery "Robbery") | 411 |

| Property crimes | |

| **Notes**   \*Number of reported crimes per 100,000 population.   2017 population: 74,532  **Source**: [2017 Neighborhood Scout](https://www.neighborhoodscout.com/nj/camden/crime) | |

[Morgan Quitno Press](/wiki/Morgan_Quitno_Press "Morgan Quitno Press") has ranked Camden as one of the top ten most dangerous cities in the United States since 1998, when they first included cities with populations less than 100,000. Camden was ranked as the third-most dangerous city in 2002, and the most dangerous city overall in 2004 and 2005.[[191]](#cite_note-:17-191)[[192]](#cite_note-:21-192) It improved to the fifth spot for the 2006 and 2007 rankings but rose to number two in 2008[[193]](#cite_note-:23-193)[[194]](#cite_note-:24-194)[[195]](#cite_note-:25-195) and to the most dangerous spot in 2009.[[196]](#cite_note-:26-196) Morgan Quitno based its rankings on crime statistics reported to the [Federal Bureau of Investigation](/wiki/Federal_Bureau_of_Investigation "Federal Bureau of Investigation") in six categories: murder, rape, robbery, aggravated assault, burglary, and auto theft.[[197]](#cite_note-:27-197) In 2011 in *[The Nation](/wiki/The_Nation "The Nation")*, journalist [Chris Hedges](/wiki/Chris_Hedges "Chris Hedges") described Camden as "the physical refuse of postindustrial America",[[198]](#cite_note-:28-198) plagued with homelessness, drug trafficking, prostitution, robbery, looting, constant violence, and an overwhelmed police force (which in 2011 lost nearly half of its officers to budget-related layoffs).[[199]](#cite_note-:29-199)

On October 29, 2012, the FBI announced Camden was ranked first in violent crime per capita of cities with over 50,000 residents.[[200]](#cite_note-Flint-200)

There were 23 homicides in Camden in 2017, the lowest since 1987 and almost half as many as the 44 murders the previous year. Both homicides and non-fatal shootings have declined sharply since 2012, when there were a record 67 homicides in the city.[[201]](#cite_note-:30-201) In 2020, there were, again, 23 homicides reported. Though 2021 also saw 23 homicides, there was, otherwise, a further reduction in violent crime, contrasting national trends.[[202]](#cite_note-:31-202)

In 2018, the [Camden County Police Department](/wiki/Camden_County_Police_Department "Camden County Police Department") reported that violent crime had dropped 18%, led by a 21% decline in aggravated assaults; overall nonviolent crimes fell by 12%, the number of arson incidents fell by 29%, burglaries by 21%, and non-fatal "shooting hit incidents" had dropped by 15%.[[33]](#cite_note-WHYY-33) In 2018, 2019, and 2020, there were 22, 24, and 23 homicides respectively.

Total violent crime in the city declined in 2022, despite 28 murders and a spike of 29% in non-violent crime, highlighted by a sharp increase in car-related crime.[[203]](#cite_note-:33-203)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Public transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Public transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Riverline_At_Walter_Rand.jpg/250px-Riverline_At_Walter_Rand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Riverline_At_Walter_Rand.jpg)

[River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(NJ_Transit) "River Line (NJ Transit)") at Walter Rand, a light rail system connecting Camden and [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey")

The [Walter Rand Transportation Center](/wiki/Walter_Rand_Transportation_Center "Walter Rand Transportation Center") opened in May 1989 under the name of Camden Transportation Center and was renamed in 1994 for former New Jersey State Senator [Walter Rand](/wiki/Walter_Rand "Walter Rand"). The surface level bus transfer center located on the corner of Martin Luther King Boulevard and Broadway, includes both indoor and outdoor stations and runs between the hours of 6am and 9:30pm, seven days a week.[[204]](#cite_note-NJ_TRANSIT-204) A majority of buses that stop at the center are [NJ Transit buses](/wiki/NJ_Transit "NJ Transit") that provide inexpensive and quick transportation to Philadelphia, Camden and Burlington Counties surrounding cities. The different routes include [313](/wiki/313_(New_Jersey_bus) "313 (New Jersey bus)"), [315](/wiki/315_(New_Jersey_bus) "315 (New Jersey bus)"), [316](/wiki/316_(New_Jersey_bus) "316 (New Jersey bus)"), [317](/wiki/317_(New_Jersey_bus) "317 (New Jersey bus)"), [400](/wiki/400_(New_Jersey_bus) "400 (New Jersey bus)"), [401](/wiki/401_(New_Jersey_bus) "401 (New Jersey bus)"), [402](/wiki/402_(New_Jersey_bus) "402 (New Jersey bus)"), [403](/wiki/403_(New_Jersey_bus) "403 (New Jersey bus)"), [404](/wiki/404_(New_Jersey_bus) "404 (New Jersey bus)"), [405](/wiki/405_(New_Jersey_bus) "405 (New Jersey bus)"), [406](/wiki/406_(New_Jersey_bus) "406 (New Jersey bus)"), [407](/wiki/407_(New_Jersey_bus) "407 (New Jersey bus)"), [408](/wiki/408_(New_Jersey_bus) "408 (New Jersey bus)"), [409](/wiki/409_(New_Jersey_bus) "409 (New Jersey bus)"), [410](/wiki/410_(New_Jersey_bus) "410 (New Jersey bus)"), [412](/wiki/412_(New_Jersey_bus) "412 (New Jersey bus)"), [413](/wiki/413_(New_Jersey_bus) "413 (New Jersey bus)"), [418](/wiki/418_(New_Jersey_bus) "418 (New Jersey bus)"), [419](/wiki/419_(New_Jersey_bus) "419 (New Jersey bus)"), [450](/wiki/450_(New_Jersey_bus) "450 (New Jersey bus)"), [451](/wiki/451_(New_Jersey_bus) "451 (New Jersey bus)"), [452](/wiki/452_(New_Jersey_bus) "452 (New Jersey bus)"), [453](/wiki/453_(New_Jersey_bus) "453 (New Jersey bus)"), [457](/wiki/457_(New_Jersey_bus) "457 (New Jersey bus)") and [551](/wiki/List_of_New_Jersey_Transit_bus_routes_(550-599) "List of New Jersey Transit bus routes (550-599)").[[205]](#cite_note-205) Depending on distance and route the bus fare varies from under a dollar for closer stops, up to fifty dollars for farther stations including Philadelphia and [Atlantic City](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"). Along with the NJ Transit buses, the center is also home to many [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") routes, that provide transportation to neighboring cities as well as to destinations all around the country, with fares varying based on the destination. Along with the bus stops, the center is home to two rail road system stations, the Walter Rand River Line station and [PATCO, Broadway station](/wiki/Broadway_station_(PATCO) "Broadway station (PATCO)") which provides access to the buses from the surrounding area.

Since opening in March 2004, NJ Transit's [River Line](/wiki/River_Line_(NJ_Transit) "River Line (NJ Transit)") has offered light rail service to cities along the Delaware River starting in North Camden, and terminating in [Trenton](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"). There are four stations located in the city, the southern most station is located at Freedom Mortgage Pavilion located on the Delaware River, and goes north along the river up to Trenton.[[206]](#cite_note-206) The second and third most south stations in Camden stop at the Camden Adventure Aquarium and at Rutgers University. The last stop in the city, the Walter Rand Transportation Center, located on Martin Luther King Boulevard and Broadway, is a major transportation hub where the PATCO, NJ Transit buses, and Greyhounds all meet.[[207]](#cite_note-207)

The [PATCO Speedline](/wiki/PATCO_Speedline "PATCO Speedline") offers frequent 24-hour train service to Philadelphia and the suburbs to the east in Camden County, including Camden, Collingswood, Haddon Township, Haddonfield, Cherry Hill, Voorhees and Lindenwold.[[208]](#cite_note-208) Throughout the two states there are a total of 13 stations. Unlike most major US transit systems, the PACTO Speedline is running 24-hours a day. Opening in 1926 under the name of The Delaware River Bridge Commission, the rail consisted of six stops in Philadelphia stops and two in Camden, [City Hall](/wiki/City_Hall_station_(PATCO) "City Hall station (PATCO)"), and [Broadway station](/wiki/Broadway_station_(PATCO) "Broadway station (PATCO)"). The first station after crossing the river into Philadelphia, Franklin Square closed in 1979 because of the low number of riders. The station was proposed for a remodel and is planned on opening during the summer of 2024.[[209]](#cite_note-209)

The [RiverLink Ferry](/wiki/RiverLink_Ferry "RiverLink Ferry") opened March 1992, as a passenger ferry service that crossed the Delaware River connecting the [Camden Waterfront](/wiki/Camden_Waterfront "Camden Waterfront") with Philadelphia's, [Penn's Landing](/wiki/Penn%27s_Landing "Penn's Landing"). The ferry operates daily from May through September, and on Fridays through Sundays in April and October.[[210]](#cite_note-210) Docking at Wiggins Park, located between the [Adventure Aquarium](/wiki/Adventure_Aquarium "Adventure Aquarium") and the [Battleship New Jersey](/wiki/Battleship_New_Jersey_Museum_and_Memorial "Battleship New Jersey Museum and Memorial"), the ferry provides access to the Adventure Aquarium, Battleship New Jersey, Camden's Children's Garden, and the [Freedom Mortgage Pavilion](/wiki/Freedom_Mortgage_Pavilion "Freedom Mortgage Pavilion").[[211]](#cite_note-211) On the Philadelphia side of the river the ferry docks at the [Independence Seaport Museum](/wiki/Independence_Seaport_Museum "Independence Seaport Museum"), and provides access to the many attractions located at Penns Landing, including multiple city piers, and restaurants as well as Museums, the battle ship Olympia, and the submarine Becuna.[[212]](#cite_note-212) Round trip ticket prices range from $8 for children and seniors to $10 for adults while children under the age of four ride for free.[[213]](#cite_note-213)

The [RiverLink Ferry](/wiki/RiverLink_Ferry "RiverLink Ferry") was not the first ferry to call Camden home. Since the start of the United States there has been a need to transport goods and people across the Delaware. Before the construction of the [Benjamin Franklin Bridge](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Bridge "Benjamin Franklin Bridge"), there were multiple ferries that launched from Camdens waterfront including on Market street, and Vine street located in the downtown area, as well as Kaighn Avenue located in South Camden. During the winter when the river had frozen horse were used to tow the ferries across like sleds, which helped slow the number of injuries and death that occurred from individuals that walked across the ice. On one occasion the fire started aboard one of the ferries. On March 15, 1856, the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") was full of floating chucks of ice rushing through the rough current when flames burst out along the upper deck. People on board did their best to calm the fire with buckets of freezing water but resorted to jumping over board.[[214]](#cite_note-214) As a result, over sixty people died in the accident with countless missing while only 30 made it out alive. Between the years of 1727 and 1766 more than 800 slaves were brought by three different ferries and sold in Downtown, Camden.[[215]](#cite_note-215) Today there are historical signs placed at these three sites to commemorate the people sold there.[[216]](#cite_note-216)

Eastbound on [I-76](/wiki/Interstate_76_(Ohio%E2%80%93New_Jersey) "Interstate 76 (Ohio–New Jersey)") at [I-676](/wiki/Interstate_676 "Interstate 676") in Camden

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Ben_Franklin_Bridge_at_sunrise_2009-09-02_06-08-46_4w.jpg/250px-Ben_Franklin_Bridge_at_sunrise_2009-09-02_06-08-46_4w.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ben_Franklin_Bridge_at_sunrise_2009-09-02_06-08-46_4w.jpg)

The [Benjamin Franklin Bridge](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Bridge "Benjamin Franklin Bridge") at sunrise, connecting Camden (right) with [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")

As of May 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit), the city had a total of 181.92 miles (292.77 km) of roadways, of which 147.54 miles (237.44 km) were maintained by the municipality, 25.39 miles (40.86 km) by Camden County, 6.60 miles (10.62 km) by the [New Jersey Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_Jersey_Department_of_Transportation "New Jersey Department of Transportation") and 2.39 miles (3.85 km) by the [Delaware River Port Authority](/wiki/Delaware_River_Port_Authority "Delaware River Port Authority").[[217]](#cite_note-217)

[Interstate 676](/wiki/Interstate_676 "Interstate 676")[[218]](#cite_note-218) and [U.S. Route 30](/wiki/U.S._Route_30_in_New_Jersey "U.S. Route 30 in New Jersey")[[219]](#cite_note-219) run through Camden to the [Benjamin Franklin Bridge](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Bridge "Benjamin Franklin Bridge") on the north side of the city. [Interstate 76](/wiki/Interstate_76_(Ohio%E2%80%93New_Jersey) "Interstate 76 (Ohio–New Jersey)") passes through briefly and interchanges with Interstate 676.[[220]](#cite_note-220)

[Route 168](/wiki/New_Jersey_Route_168 "New Jersey Route 168") passes through briefly in the south[[221]](#cite_note-221) and County Routes [537](/wiki/County_Route_537_(New_Jersey) "County Route 537 (New Jersey)"),[[222]](#cite_note-222) [543](/wiki/County_Route_543_(New_Jersey) "County Route 543 (New Jersey)"),[[223]](#cite_note-223) [551](/wiki/County_Route_551_(New_Jersey) "County Route 551 (New Jersey)")[[224]](#cite_note-224) and [561](/wiki/County_Route_561_(New_Jersey) "County Route 561 (New Jersey)")[[225]](#cite_note-225) all travel through the center of the city.

## Environmental problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Environmental problems")]

Main article: [Environmental issues in Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Camden,_New_Jersey "Environmental issues in Camden, New Jersey")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
