Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Camden,_New_Jersey "Special:EditPage/Camden, New Jersey") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(November 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Camden has faced many environmental problems due to its history of heavy industry and the improper disposal of contaminants. Environmental concerns include air/water pollution and soil contamination. There are several [Superfund](/wiki/Superfund "Superfund") sites throughout the city. In recent years, illegal dumping has become an issue due to the large number of vacant lots throughout the city and a lack of security and maintenance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

At the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Camden was the [14th-most populous municipality in the state](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_New_Jersey "List of municipalities in New Jersey"),[[247]](#cite_note-Largest2020-247) with a population of 71,791,[[9]](#cite_note-Census2020-9)[[10]](#cite_note-LWD2020-10) a decrease of 5,553 (−7.2%) from the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") count of 77,344, when it was the 12th-largest in the state by population, falling behind both [Brick Township](/wiki/Brick_Township,_New_Jersey "Brick Township, New Jersey") and nearby [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey"),[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240)[[242]](#cite_note-LWD2010-242) which in turn reflected a decline of 1,984 (-2.5%) from the 79,318 counted in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census").[[239]](#cite_note-Quick2000-239)[[248]](#cite_note-248) The [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")'s [Population Estimates Program](/wiki/Population_Estimates_Program "Population Estimates Program") calculated a population of 71,100 for 2023, making it the [532nd-most populous municipality](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the nation.[[11]](#cite_note-ANNRNK-11)

### 2010 census

The [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") counted 77,344 people, 24,475 households, and 16,912 families in the city. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 8,669.6 per square mile (3,347.4/km2). There were 28,358 housing units at an average density of 3,178.7 per square mile (1,227.3/km2). The racial makeup was 17.59% (13,602) [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._census) "White (U.S. census)"), 48.07% (37,180) [Black or African American](/wiki/Black_(U.S._census) "Black (U.S. census)"), 0.76% (588) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._census) "Native American (U.S. census)"), 2.12% (1,637) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._census) "Asian (U.S. census)"), 0.06% (48) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. census)"), 27.57% (21,323) from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census#Race "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 3.83% (2,966) from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._census) "Hispanic (U.S. census)") of any race were 47.04% (36,379) of the population.[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240) The Hispanic population of 36,379 was the tenth-highest of any municipality in New Jersey and the proportion of 47.0% was the state's 16th-highest percentage.[[250]](#cite_note-250)[[251]](#cite_note-251) The Puerto Rican population was 30.7%.[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240)

Of the 24,475 households, 37.9% had children under the age of 18; 22.3% were married couples living together; 37.9% had a female householder with no husband present and 30.9% were non-families. Of all households, 24.8% were made up of individuals and 7.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 3.02 and the average family size was 3.56.[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240)

31.0% of the population were under the age of 18, 13.1% from 18 to 24, 28.0% from 25 to 44, 20.3% from 45 to 64, and 7.6% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 28.5 years. For every 100 females, the population had 94.7 males. For every 100 females ages 18 and older there were 91.0 males.[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240)

The city of Camden was 47% Hispanic of any race, 44% non-Hispanic black, 6% non-Hispanic white, and 3% other. Camden is predominately populated by African Americans and Puerto Ricans.[[240]](#cite_note-Census2010-240)

The Census Bureau's 2006–2010 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") showed that (in 2010 [inflation-adjusted](/wiki/Inflation_adjustment "Inflation adjustment") dollars) [median household income](/wiki/Median_household_income "Median household income") was $27,027 (with a margin of error of +/− $912) and the median family income was $29,118 (+/− $1,296). Males had a median income of $27,987 (+/− $1,840) versus $26,624 (+/− $1,155) for females. The per capita income for the city was $12,807 (+/− $429). About 33.5% of families and 36.1% of the population were below the poverty line, including 50.3% of those under age 18 and 26.2% of those age 65 or over.[[252]](#cite_note-252)

As of 2006, 52% of the city's residents lived in poverty, one of the highest rates in the nation.[[253]](#cite_note-253) The city had a median household income of $18,007, the lowest of all U.S. communities with populations of more than 65,000 residents.[[254]](#cite_note-254) A group of poor Camden residents were the subject of a *[20/20](/wiki/20/20_(US_television_series) "20/20 (US television series)")* special on poverty in America broadcast on January 26, 2007, in which [Diane Sawyer](/wiki/Diane_Sawyer "Diane Sawyer") profiled the lives of three young children growing up in Camden.[[255]](#cite_note-255) A follow-up was shown on November 9, 2007.[[256]](#cite_note-256)

In 2011, Camden's unemployment rate was 19.6%, compared with 10.6% in Camden County as a whole.[[257]](#cite_note-257) As of 2009, the unemployment rate in Camden was 19.2%, compared to the 10% overall unemployment rate for Burlington, Camden and Gloucester counties and a rate of 8.4% in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and the four surrounding counties in [Southeastern Pennsylvania](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley").[[258]](#cite_note-258)

## Points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Points of interest")]

* [Corinne's Place](/wiki/Corinne%27s_Place "Corinne's Place") is a Black-owned soul food restaurant located in Camden, New Jersey. Corinne Bradley-Powers opened the restaurant on Haddon Avenue in 1989.[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63)
* [Adventure Aquarium](/wiki/Adventure_Aquarium "Adventure Aquarium") – Originally opened in 1992, it re-opened in its current form in May 2005 featuring about 8,000 animals living in varied forms of semi-aquatic, freshwater and marine habitats. The Adventure aquarium is also the only aquarium in the world that houses [hippopotamuses.](/wiki/Hippopotamus "Hippopotamus")[[259]](#cite_note-259)
* [Waterfront Music Pavilion](/wiki/BB%26T_Pavilion "BB&T Pavilion") – An outdoor [amphitheater](/wiki/Amphitheater "Amphitheater")/indoor theater complex with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of 25,000. Formerly known as the Susquehanna Bank Center.
* [Battleship New Jersey Museum and Memorial](/wiki/Battleship_New_Jersey_Museum_and_Memorial "Battleship New Jersey Museum and Memorial") – Opened in October 2001, providing access to the battleship [USS *New Jersey*](/wiki/USS_New_Jersey_(BB-62) "USS New Jersey (BB-62)") that had been towed to the Camden area for restoration in 1999.[[260]](#cite_note-260)
* [Harleigh Cemetery](/wiki/Harleigh_Cemetery,_Camden "Harleigh Cemetery, Camden") – Established in 1885, the cemetery is the burial site of [Walt Whitman](/wiki/Walt_Whitman "Walt Whitman"), several Congressmen and many other South Jersey notables.[[261]](#cite_note-261)
* [Walt Whitman House](/wiki/Walt_Whitman_House "Walt Whitman House")
* [National Register of Historic Places listings in Camden County, New Jersey](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Camden_County,_New_Jersey "National Register of Historic Places listings in Camden County, New Jersey")

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Camden,_New_Jersey&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The fictional Camden mayor Carmine Polito in the 2013 film *[American Hustle](/wiki/American_Hustle "American Hustle")* is loosely based on 1970s Camden mayor [Angelo Errichetti](/wiki/Angelo_Errichetti "Angelo Errichetti").[[262]](#cite_note-262)

The 1995 film *[12 Monkeys](/wiki/12_Monkeys "12 Monkeys")* contains scenes on Camden's [Admiral Wilson Boulevard](/wiki/Admiral_Wilson_Boulevard "Admiral Wilson Boulevard").[[263]](#cite_note-263)

Under this form of government, the City Council consisted of seven (7) Council members originally all elected at-large. In 1994, the City opted to modify the form of government to better address the changing needs of the citizenry. To that end, the City of Camden was divided into four (4) councilmanic districts, instead of electing the entire Council at-large. One Council member was elected from each of the four (4) districts."
